reviews.review_text:
Devs are hostile against modding, the free cosmetics are frequently FOMO "limited time only" drops that have no reason not to be opted into at any time, the paid cosmetics are absurdly expensive, and I've heard a lot of bad things about the DLCs.

Also they have the lesbian flag in the pride bundle but not the gay flag? Why are they excluding gay men?

Quite unique and interesting game. Enjoyed it pretty much, many things changed since early access in a best way.
Not quite enjoyed quest system, but overall it's decent.

This has got to be one of the most clunky, unintuitive games I have played in ages. The tutorial/onboarding needs to be WAY better. I highly suggest the devs sit down with some people that have never played the game before, give them no input, and see what the players experience is like.

An amazing game, but watch your base size!

I'm a veteran player of this game, with more than 600 hours of gameplay split across 3 different Xbox accounts that are all mine.

I didn't experience many crashes on console, but 2 of my worlds got corrupted after reaching around 128MB in size. It's really hard to hit that limit, but you lose everything if you do. I hope they've optimized it by now. I just got back into the game because I finally bought my first PC!

Performance-wise, my new setup holds at around 30 FPS with everything at maximum settings when I'm inside a massive base with maybe too many items. However, if I drop the settings down to medium or low, I can easily get about 120 FPS, and sometimes around 80 FPS on high.
Great game, just wished the devs fixed gamebreaking bugs quicker instead of focusing on less pressing issues.
Very fun. play with other people. it makes the game so much better. and put a hydrazene thruster on your medium buggy.

It's a pretty well made game, it's sandbox, it's visually appealing to some groups of people and also getting the achievements are sometimes so rewarding mentally. (A quick way to get possibly an infinite source of power for those starting out is tapper, auto arm, small generator).
Fun spacey sandbox game. Just watch for the cross-platform servers. sometimes. you fall through the world. Otherwise, super fun game to play if you have kids that like exploring in video games.

Generally I think this game does fall a little short on a lot of its promises, either due to some janky controls, poor pacing and balancing, and maybe an unclear idea of what Astroneer felt like it wanted to be vs what it was. This isn't a game where you're expected to build a massive base, or build machines to extract resources to enable you to explore. Everything you can automate, you can usually gather by hand faster, easier, and cheaper, especially later in the game.

But I won't lie, it did completely take my attention away for several days, and while it didn't entirely satisfy my craving for a lite space exploration type crafting game, it did deliver on giving me a solid goal to work towards that I enjoyed working on.

I'd say my biggest issues was the UI constantly fighting me, either from the controls control or lack of readability. I strongly feel a significant portion of my playtime would have been cut if the technology and crafting menus had categories like "Power, Storage, Vehicles" etc.

Bytes also feel poorly implemented, a lot of technology in this game is gatekept from materials you don't have immediate access to, and I think that serves a strong enough reason to go explore. Bytes are a currency you get from scanning random things for a quick boost, to hauling samples for a machine to scan and generate bytes over time. You spend these bytes to unlock new things, and the costs get so high I found myself more gatekept by a timer to unlocking new technology, than the materials I needed to make them at all. It just felt a little too artifical for my tastes, and I wish bytes were just used to unlock decorative or very niche-use items.

But my favorite things was what all that material grinding builds up to. Exploring the decently varied planets in various vehicles, grabbing materials to bring back home and process into bigger, newer things. It's a satisfying game loop when it works, and I'd say it just works enough I can recommend it.
Very exciting to play, there's ALOT of content. Overall this is my favourite game to play!

nice game, should buy. Occasionally new big updates and really sympathetic game. Really fun solo or with friends. lot of gameplay too, exploring + nice and original mechanics to stay alive and develop the base. Worth the price.
Fun game the only problem is the exess bugs from megatech but mostly fixed
Great game to play with your friends or on your own, buggy rarely but pretty stable for the most part. Would highly recommend, best cheap game I think I've played so far!!
9/10 เป็นเกมที่ยอดเยี่ยมสำหรับใครก็ตามที่ต้องการหลีกหนีจากเกมแนว Competitive
Extremely fun co-op game. Adventuring is super fun and there are so many things to build!
Beautiful planets, fun exploration, and a unique mystery. What more could you want?
I haven't played much, but i like games atmosphere. It is chill and relaxing with enough puzzleness to keep brain interested. Just beautiful landscapes.
I have owned this game for a decade, every 2-3 yrs il do a new playthrough. unique game that scratches that "itch", but does die relatively quick no real end game or goals other than just sandbox.

I really think this is one of the best space exploration and factory type games out there. The combination of exploring a new world each times and manufacturing resources feels euphoric and honestly very relaxing at times. I would definitely get this game if you enjoy games like Satisfactory or other factory type games, or games that involve space travel like EVE or No Mans Sky. While Astroneer has a much smaller world than those 2, it still feels expansive in the space it has.

My thoughts on this game a little complicated, I love it but... I'm gonna list the cons because they are horrendously notable, and it'd feel disingenuous not to mention them before mentioning what I like about the game.

- You need a good PC to run this game. Mine's capable of running most modern games with minimal issues, I boot up Astroneer and I hear the fans start running like crazy.
- The power system completely sucks. There's very little point of running early-midgame Solar/Wind energy as anything more than a supplemental boost, you will be running carbon generators to get 90% of your energy gen.
- The voxel grid is as clunky as it looks. If you want to have a flat-leveled base... tough luck, it's not happening without getting a specific upgrade.
- The camera is straightup dogwater in caves, trying to mine something out of a cave roof is incredibly obnoxious and I don't recommend it.

However, on the good side!
+ It does a LOT with a low amount of content. You can play the game for hours on end, despite there not being - many usable items, you'll still be able to pour plenty of time into it.
+ AFAIK the game still receives updates, the recent Megatech DLC while not adding too much to the game, also came with free additions for people who did not buy the DLC.
+ It's good both solo and with friends, also enjoyable at any pace.
+ The progression is straightforward and the game actually provides a resource guide, telling you exactly what you'll find on other planets, there's a few things the game doesn't tell you, but you can figure them out through experimentation.
+ It feels incredibly casual, nothing's too complicated, I can figure out how to do mostly anything I want without having to put in too much effort. Plain great on that side of things.
+ It's Moddable! There aren't many mods for it, but it does have mods. Most of which fix a few issues like the fact that more expensive power systems aren't worth using, or there being no REAL automation due to the fact that a lot of resources are actually non-renewable.
+ The research system doesn't suck. Many games have research systems, most of them feel tedious and slow, Astroneer's is paced properly, it also helps to note that you can put quite a lot of things into a research station, most important being the gas materials, you can in fact make an infinite research farm with an atmospheric condenser.

A few nitpicks:

- This is not really an automation game. Every resource besides Organic (And with Megatech, anything that can be made in a soil centrifuge) is temporary, auto extractors do give you extra out of it (and quite a lot extra), but will run out inevitably. What automation does exist almost always requires manual input.
- Cable Extenders cost copper, you only get five, and the range is abysmally short. Why? At least give ten.
- Storage and placement is clunky and annoying, due to everything having a physical in-world slot to be placed, as well as the weird polygonal grid everything functions on. There's also no real base-building in the game, which is unfortunate, you will be outdoors pretty much at all times.

And that's pretty much all I've got, I recommend it, most of my issues are just nitpicks and pretty easy to ignore, if you like the look of the game, get it, you can leave the first planet within an hour if you know what you're doing, that'll tell you enough about the gameplay for you to decide for yourself if you want to keep playing or refund.

on my Play Station 4 account I have over 1200 hours on this game. this game was genuinely my childhood. I remember playing this all the way back in 2018 for the first time. I fell in love right away. there is just something about this game that brings peace and happiness to my soul. I dont think there is anything more relaxing then equipping portable oxygenator, and just jogging around the planet while listening to the nostalgic sound track. the end missions in this game genuinely turned my bad days upside down. no matter how bad of a day I had, I could always jump on Astroneer and flip my day around. (pro tip to make the game more memorable, save the EVA missions for last, first complete everything else, then move on to it). this game made and shaped me into the person I am today. now I know how that must sound, but it really did. it made me realise that even when you are the loneliest person in the solar system. you can still find happiness where ever you go
I mean, yeah, its a grate game and all. Yeah. But I ain't spending any time on it... Every three months someone is torn to pieces by a crocodile in North Queensland
Excellent exploration and base building game.

I you are the one who loves Sci-fi and exploring the universe then this game is for you. Here you are exploring a completely new Galaxy with different plants and trienng to survive by creating new resources for future settlements to arrive on those plantes. The grapchis are a bit kidish but it is lovely to play the game. only drawback is that if there were options to play with other players in Co-op to build the base and stuff then it would be better.
The game is terrifyingly fun and satisfying to play 😂 Highly recommend it (Especially for nerdy grinders like me)

Quick Description:
-Astroneer is a space exploration sandbox survival crafting adventure.  The first few hours are rough.  I actually uninstalled the game three times in my first 10 hrs playing, but fortunately, I stubbornly pushed through.  This game is phenomenal once you get comfortable with the UI, understand the basics, and start setting up complex logistical systems.  The mid to late game is extremely fun and gives the player tons of freedom to find solutions to problems.  I loved exploring every planet, finding secrets, and unlocking the strongest tools in the game.  The tutorial is atrocious and late game is pretty underwhelming.  Yes, you are given tons of freedom to set up megabases, but the game doesn't offer many tangible goals to strive for.  

Consensus:
-Excellent Game (Tier 2 and makes up 25% of my reviews).  I was very close to giving this my highest score, but Astroneer just misses the mark when it comes to giving the player direction and long term goals.  Satisfactory is a masterclass in giving the player freedom to be creative, but also nudging them toward rewarding goals.  I just wish Astroneer had a few more clear goals to achieve.  Setting up a massive base with a complex automation system is really fun, but there is no real need to.  Sandbox fans will probably adore this game for it's creativity and the freedom it's gives players.  But if you are looking for a little more structure, you might be disappointed.

I very much enjoyed playing Astroneer. It was slow to start, but once I got going, I played until I reach what I consider the end. I wont say what that is, but you can keep playing after you've completed the game. Not sure if I will. 88 hours is a good slog, and it might be time for something else.

many many many many potential hours of play. i have this on console and pc. its one of the few good games that you can play for many months or years. put it down for a year or two, come back and easily continue where you left off.  If you like hoarding resources... this game is for you.  and you can play with others (just be careful of letting someone you don't know play on a world you've spent years on, they can wreck it in no time). best to start a new coop world with someone if you want to play with people. i have all the expansions, but I've not played any of them yet.
If you want to pretend like you got off this forsaken Earth and can finally be left alone to your devices, Astroneer is the game for you. A+++++ would buy again.
Amazing game, I've had so many relaxing late night sessions playing and it's so peaceful. May feel like a grind at times but I always come back to play. 10/10

Highly reccomend, especially with a friend.

One of the best space exploration games. The way you find out how the game operates, how to use the different gadgets and the secrets around the world feels very organic. It also ends right when it should, there's just enough planets to be interesting but not to many where it would be tedious to explore them all. 

Banger.

Great game to play with friends or solo. Many great updates over the years. ASTRONEER has come a long way since Early Access 10 years ago. Some turbulence with the Megatech update but overall still going strong. Nothing too complicated yet nothing too simple either.

Genuinely one of the best games out there. I love the fact that you will almost never completely finish playing Astroneer, as there's infinite possible outcomes and ways how to start or end your journey. I also love that the coop system works really smooth! Definitely worth buying.
This Game is so at-your-own-pace that it seems too good to be true. I enjoy the options of craftables along with the space exploration very much.
This is what happens when a factory simulator meets a space exploration game. True fun.
it is a really good game. but i do think the dlc could use some improving when it comes to them being buggy.

Even with my laptop, the game runs very ok unless i had a lot of things in one single place but, the movement is the only thing that sometimes fails but everything aside that is ok, the music, landscapes, mecanincs, "lore", i definetly recommend playing this alone first and then play it with friends. 8/10.

Astroneer is a fantastic game. The early game is a lot of resource-gathering and the progression feels very balanced. The mid-game can feel a bit tedious, but overall feels good. I haven't gotten to the late-game, so I can't speak to that. 

This is a game I will continue to put hours into for years to come. The procedurally-generated worlds make every playthrough different. The in-game wiki (Astropedia) is fantastic and has so much information in it. It is easily accessible through the "pause" menu and the layout is great. 

I can pick this game up again after a year of not playing and it still feels just as great as it did when I first picked it up. The game is gorgeous and the terraforming aspect and lack of enemies (other than hazardous plants) sets it apart from other games in the survival-craft genre. It is a very chill game to play and it's one I reach for when I need a quieter, calmer game to play. 

Overall, this game is fantastic and I highly, highly recommend to anyone who enjoys space-themed games, survival-crafts, and resource gathering. There is no tutorial, so using the Astropedia during the first playthrough is paramount.

It's a very chill game, wandering around gathering things, figuring out the mechanics for how to make some semblance of automation for your "factories".  I'm giving it a recommendation mainly because I'm a parent of young kids, who at the end of the day, generally doesn't want something to also get me amped up.  A game like this, where you're very casually building your world is somewhat relaxing.  However, I found myself after about 50 hours just kinda going: ...that's it -- jumpstart an artifact on each planet, drill to the core, jumpstart the core... and repeat a few times?  At that point it just started feeling tedious vs. something like Satisfactory, where I genuinely took pleasure in building out the world.  I'm not sure if it's just the mechanics that are putting me off or what, but I find this game easy to put down, but not so easy I wouldn't recommend it because I can see some of the appeal.
Very cute little exploration/factory game. The automation could use some work, but the physics aspect of being on tiny planets that you can dig straight through really makes it unique.

I played this game a ton when I was younger on console with my brother, it was quite fun! Still holds up just as well today, there's lots of planets to explore, good progression and updates to this day. Nowadays I can never quite stick with open world survival games long enough to get very far but if that interest ever comes back this will definitely be one of the first ones I complete. I recommend at market price.
Runs good on almost any hardwer. I love the buildig system and a fun game in general.
Game is cool and fun until your friend dig the dirt below your rover and send it to Ohio.

An alright chill game to play unfocused or with friends. The late game is sort of unsatisfying and the automation feels half baked.
Super fun and unique game; even more fun with friends. Singleplayer or co-op private worlds. GOATed game imo yall gotta get it fr
It's fun, the progression is pretty straight forward but most of the challenge comes from figuring out how to do the stuff.
One of my favorite games. I think I've spent hours driving around in my buggy or that circle from Jurassic Park. Beautiful soundtrack and very satisfying when you complete a goal.

Astroneer:

Is a great relaxing survival and exploration game, and I had a lot of fun with it.

The game is all about exploring strange planets, gathering resources, building bases, crafting vehicles, and slowly expanding what you can do. The terrain tool is one of the best parts, because you can dig, shape, and change the world around you almost like clay. It makes exploring caves, building paths, and setting up your base feel very satisfying.

The gameplay loop is fun and addictive. You land on a planet, collect resources, unlock new items, build better equipment, travel to new planets, and keep pushing further. It is not a harsh survival game with hunger or thirst, so it feels more relaxing than stressful. The visuals, music, and general atmosphere also make it a nice game to play at your own pace.

The story is not the main focus, but the mystery and planet exploration give the game enough direction to keep things interesting. Going for 100% completion was fun and fairly easy compared to many other survival games. It gives you goals without making the achievement hunt feel painful.

I would recommend Astroneer to casual players, achievement hunters, story players, survival fans, and anyone who enjoys relaxing space exploration games.

Overall, Astroneer is a fun, creative, and relaxing game that is easy to recommend.

such a nice and relaxing game, easy to run on just about any device. even runs on my laptop so I can play on the go either at school, in vacation and even more.
I personnaly recommend playing on controller but that might just be because I played 100 hours on xbox before getting it on pc.
would recommend to just about anyone looking for a nice, relaxing and kinda long game that doesnt require any tryhard
It is a very fun little game with unique gameplay and ATROX :0
I bought this game twice just to give System Era more money, cause I love this game and its amazing and runs beautifully and lets me do stupid stuff
do NOT dig right after playing. you WILL run out of oxygen. if you just follow the quests when starting, you'll actually get good
Incredibly vibe heavy space game in all the best ways. Great music, art style, and sound direction, If you like survival crafts you will like this game.

stratches the autism itch BUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUUT
i hope you like a camera thats worst than every ps2 game combined because ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ it rips it away from your control constantly or just zoomz into your ass because your camera went past a crumb too close to you

I used to love watching videos about this game in 2017, now that I've finally played it, I've realised it's extremely disappointing. 

Pros:
It's an open world survival crafter that you can play with friends. The tech progression is nice and there's a decent variety of vehicles and base building/automation that you can experiment with. 

Cons:
There is no real direction with this game, no helpful tutorial, no mobile enemies, frustrating inventory management, annoying UI, a janky 3rd person camera that will drive you nuts, and horrible clunky movement settings that makes every surface feel like skating on ice. Even though it's a multiplayer game, there isn't much to do. You are just crafting stuff for the sake of crafting stuff. It's like playing Subnautica but there's no fish.

The 'story/lore' relies on you activating Gateway Chamber's on every planet, an identical process that you have to repeat 34 TIMES across 7 planets!! And then you have to get to the Gateway Engine's of each planet by drilling to the core, figure out what blocks you need to create their Geometric Triptych, drill and go back to the surface, return with the items and activate the core, do this 7 times. Then at the end of the game, for the Gateway Portal, realize you need duplicate Geometric Triptych's from each planet, so repeat the previous 7 trips again. 

After being out for almost a decade, only 8.6% of players have ever completed the game, which shows just how 'fun' the game is. This is just a boring repetitive crafting simulator game, there is no survival element. There are FAR better games in this genre. 2/10

This game lacks direction. It's also really hard to play when there is no cursor showing for you. I spent far too long trying to unlock the smelter because the cursor wouldn't show up and I couldn't open the pages for the larger structures. Waste of money for a game that has been out for 10+ years.

This game is like EA junior, you need buy DLC to really enjoy it. Unless you like to dig dirt all the time that maybe this game is for you. I had fun playing for a few but all the mission are locked behind a pay wall. You need to buy DLC just to complete one storyline. 

Even the base building suck. Seriously there is no point to create a massive base of operation on one planet, because the automation aspect in this game is useless. There is no consumible item that you need to craft for mining or any upkeep for equipments/ facilities. There are a lot of potential but maybe you need to buy the MEGATECH DLC. I dont know currently Devs been focusing on DLC rather than base game.

The best part about this game is that it was so unfun, that it made me try No Man's Sky again, which I now actually enjoy. 

Astroneer has a horrible UI, even worse inventory management, essentially all of its mechanics are unintuitive, I've had bugs that made the ground suddenly invisible, also being able to walk nowhere without spamming leash-pillars for air gets boring quick. The game is now officially 10 years old and supposedly has been getting regular content patches, but it still just feels barebones. Oh and the worst of all? WHAT ON EARTH ARE THESE CONTROLS? Holy, hell. I can't remember the last time I played something this tiltingly impractical and clunky.

Honestly? Out of all great survival games out there? Why would anybody pick this?
it is ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ that you have to play adventure mode to even be able to play the dlc you already paid for i used to love this game but now it is TRASH
i bought the game after my friends were already halfway through the game and had no idea what i was doing. I'm never starting a game late again
Good Graphics Great Music but is a tad bit confusing in the bieginning
Space is a ♥♥♥♥♥ and ♥♥♥♥ the little rattle slappers
Have died on Calidor almost every time i've gone there, now I only stay on Glacio. Novus, and Desolo. 10/10
Astroneer is just plain fun. I wish I could go back in time and discover it again.

The onboarding is terrible without a "guru" to guide you, it's just frustrating. The "immersive UI" is unintuitive and makes simple tasks like printing a total chore.

To make matters worse, the multiplayer is a mess. We kept getting disconnected, and the game is riddled with bugs. I’ve tried giving this game several chances, but it’s always the same -> it looks great, but the gameplay is boring. Base building is frustrating, exploration is pointless, and it’s just not fun. Refunded
i like being a little space guy, a little confused space guy if you will!!
Severely buggy, loaded with microtransactions and bad dlc.

nice game, i can spend all day to just digging the dirt
Game is Fun Solo it's fun together I love that they keep adding to it as well.
I have to write a review to upgrade my steam account but this game is fun i guess lol

Gonna start this comprehensive review with intense focus on a single detail: this UI is a strong contender for my favorite in all of video games, the closest competition being Death Stranding and Deep Rock Galactic. So much of it is diegetic & organically integrated, with your suit showing basic stuff like oxygen & power, smelters show the finished product being made bit-by-bit, power consumption & shortages are telegraphed via power cable colors & meters, everything that has some kind of selection interface has a dedicated screen you can click or type through. There’s so much effort put into keeping everything clean-feeling, taking everything that would be shoved into some pop-up window and giving it a more tactile presentation—you can really feel how you’d live & function in this world.

And what is that world? Well, it’s several worlds actually.

Astroneer is a sandbox pseudo-survival game where you’re a faceless astronaut fella drop podded to a planet for, I don’t know, research? World domination? You’re given a series of missions to accomplish that you can totally ignore if you’d like and just screw around with the mechanics that feel half Minecraft, half Factorio. You run around a procedurally generated world collecting resources for building cool stuff, then inevitably digging deeper to find cooler resources for building cooler stuff. Also there’s automation mechanics used in basebuilding, mostly for keeping stuff powered, refining & collecting resources, and acquiring precious research points to unlock the coolest, bestest stuff.

The biggest differences are, like I implied, there’s no real survival mechanics beyond oxygen & power; no eating or sleeping, which hey I don’t mind losing those annoyances, but no deadly fauna either. There’s some mean plants, but nothing that’ll proactively attack you unless you get too close, and your only weapon against them is erasing the dirt they’re rooted in. Also, just to be frank, the variety of what you can find exploring is rather limited, with only a few biomes to find and some of the generated treasures feeling redundant after a while. But in fairness, Astroneer was designed a certain way to direct players away from noticing this…by literally putting them on a leash.

See, I lied earlier; you really can’t just galivant about the world moments after dropping in. You start with a very limited oxygen supply, and your only source of oxygen is your starting shelter, meaning for anywhere more than a few skips away, you’ll need a line of tethers connected to your base. By keeping you tied down to that base at the start, you’ll probably end up running through the early missions right away, just to get built up and freed from your confinement. And right near the end of that starting spurt, you’ll come across the main objects for finishing the game: the Gateway Portals. You activate one portal on the surface, it unlocks the portal at the planet’s center, and once you activate that, you can teleport to the “strange satellite” that’s your fast travel between planets.

And now I have to talk about the other planets and the annoyance in that. You’re made aware of the other planets from the start—just checking your little explorer encyclopedia tells you what special resources the have, and how deadly (not) they are. So given that, and given all the base-building stuff you unlock for crafting, it’s somewhat expected you’ll build a base on each planet, which gets pretty tedious after a while. Maybe the real expectation is what I ended up doing: building bases on 4 out of 7 planets, then kitting myself out so I could go to a portal, power it, then dig straight to the center to finish it ASAP.

Because by that point, I had seen pretty much everything the game had for me to see. I mined & created every resource, unlocked everything I could with research, figured out my optimal mobile base setup, collected every snail (loved that part), and got all the most radical mobility options available. By that point, it felt like there was nothing else to really do besides beat the game. I need to harp on that, because it feels like somewhat of a critical failure; the sandbox game didn’t feel like a sandbox. The only real “sandbox” mechanics was the terrain tool, which could be used to mold out some cool areas if you think only gray colored dirt is cool. Kinda like a literal sandbox I suppose. All the actual buildables feel too purposeful & isolated in function—there’s some decorative bits, but not much beyond gussying up the pragmatic base you need for progressing.

The devs seemed to combat this endgame slump by adding a Megatech paid DLC that I did not care one bit for. They figured going harder on the automation aspect was the way to go, encouraging you to build huge bases meant to work with hundreds of resources an hour, all for…what exactly? This isn’t Factorio, which has a UI and design meant to maximize the satisfaction from making cleaner, more efficient beltways. But more importantly, it offers actual purpose to making a massive factory in the first place, not just extra bonuses that make things a little better to play a game I don’t want to play anymore. It’s a cynical attempt at using “number go up” to lengthen playtime, disregarding the strongest point of the game: the exploration.

Maybe that sounds weird when I ragged on exploration earlier for not having much to find, but it’s still the truth. Astroneer, just as the name implies, feels at its best when you’re running around, either looking for stuff or just enjoying your rad movement options. The buggy you can get at the start is already pretty fun, then there’s the gravity globe, the jetpacks, the hoverboard, the VTOL, all of which control unique from one another and feel great to use. It tempted me to build a race track just to make the most of them, but it seemed pointless to do without an opponent—multiplayer probably makes this way more fun—so I just rushed to the end of the game with them. It’s like a missed opportunity to have so little taking advantage of the really fun thing they made.

So it’s good news that they seemed to have learned their lesson. Developers System Era Softworks have their Astroneer follow-up, STARSEEKER: Astroneer Expeditions, slated for release this year. Just from the 50 second clip & screenshots on the Steam store page, they put way more effort into fleshing out exploration: more tools & even more mobility options, including a Buzz Lightyear-esque set of wings I get starry eyed looking at, actual fauna that looks biome-specific and some more hostile than others, and even underwater exploration that I never considered. It’s an exciting & reassuring direction for them to take, I might even play it on release if the word-of-mouth is strong enough. Or even if it’s not, I’ll try it anyways, as I’m confident I’ll still find more to like relative to Astroneer.

Honestly, I probably shouldn’t have bothered finishing Astroneer. It barely even has an ending—I won’t spoil it, but it wasn’t satisfying and it just threw me back to my home base with nothing to show for it. I still liked the game overall, and just to reiterate, this is still one of the absolute best UIs I’ve encountered. But when I spent a couple different times really wracking my brain, getting lost in the base-building sauce like every other survival game, and I’m thinking “I never want to play another survival game again,” well I gotta be true to those feelings. What Astroneer was versus what Astroneer needed to be is a venn diagram with too little crossover. I’m hopeful the follow-up makes a circle.

This game is so tedious with its controls and functions, it completely detracts from the actual gameplay. Also, the tutorial still sucks and clunky after 8 years. I gave up again for the third time because of this. One of the most clunkiest control games, regardless of genre, that I have ever played.

There's a great little game in there somewhere, but the lack of tutorial and absolutely awful menu navigation has buried it too deep to make the game enjoyable.  This game genre is right up my street but the UI feels like it's been ported from a Tamagotchi.
very relaxing and balanced easy to play co op

Okay, I was extremely excited for this game and even bought all of the DLC at the same time as the base game awhile before this review -- However, upon playing it, the controls are HORRIBLE. The tutorial isn't all that helpful either, and is very confusiong and borderline unintuative as a new player coming into the game with strange controls that don't make sense (and can't be rebinded as far as I could tell, but to be fair, I really did try to give the base controls their fair share of effort)

I adore space games and even more so survival craft games, and after seeing so many people play this game, I really wanted to try it myself, but I ended up refunding it as I couldn't play it with the current controls. 

I honestly may buy just the base game again simply to give myself time to screw around and actually figure things out after another update or two, as the game is still being developed even ten years after it's inital release, so maybe I just simply missed something -- I really do want to give this game another try, but as it stands with my first experience, I'd prefer if things were reworked and made more clear; Esp in the beginning of the game.

Updates sounds great, but I still can't play the game until you remove the auto-save when getting into a vehicle. It stutters worse and worse every time and makes mutliplayer so frustrating I can't even enjoy the game. PLEASE add manual save from menu and timed auto-save like 99.9% of other games.

Dont get me wrong, its a good game, but i has a lot of annoying quirks that make it hard to finish and in my opinion does not deserve a +90% rating. 
Controlls can be very cluncy at times, in tunnels its hard to look around because all you see is your own back. Keeping your base orderly feels more like a hassle than a real upgrade, but thats mostly nitpicking. 
What annoyed me most is that the planets feel very repetetive and after the 3rd which gives you access to most ressources you just go there to activate the core, which is just 5-45min of digging down, depending on how you prepeard yourself.   [spoiler] Using the cores to teleport is also so much hassel, why do I need to teleport 4 times just to switch planets? [/spoiler]

With friends, this game is a lot more fun. But if you enjoy building things, automation, or just deleting the ground, this is for you. Or, if you have trouble sleeping, the soundtrack will definitely put you to sleep.
A fun game of exploration, survival and crafting. A bit rough on the edges sometimes, but just look up a short tutorial and you will be off having fun in no time.

+ very cozy
+ nice thougth-out UI to still be immersive (power usage, train distance etc.)
+ very cool quests and progression

- occasional glitch with vehicles
- altered terrain has to load in and is slow (until exiting to main menu)
runs so smooth, you can't tell it's not butter.
A very fun game to play with friends. I love Sylvie. 10/10.
Its an awesome sandbox story survival game.
too much work just to play with my friends (which i didnt even end up doing becuase it was impossible)

I've played a little bit, and I would say its a good game.
